article_title: Nearly half of cybersecurity pros want to quit - here's why
core_thesis: The article argues that cybersecurity professionals are increasingly dissatisfied due to a mismatch between high demand for their skills and inadequate compensation and recognition, exacerbated by the rapid evolution of AI-driven threats. It suggests that to retain talent and remain effective, security staff must develop strategic and communication skills to align security with business goals, while organizations need to better appreciate and reward their cyber teams.
detailed_summary: The article, based on the Harvey Nash Global Tech Talent & Salary Report (surveying over 3,646 tech professionals), reveals that nearly half (49%) of cybersecurity professionals plan to leave their jobs within the next year, significantly above the 39% global average for tech roles. Only 29% of cyber professionals received a pay increase in the past year, compared to 56% in DevOps, 51% in product management, and 50% in business analysis. This disparity stems from a perceived complacency among boards, who often assume security is adequate because no major breach has occurred, failing to recognize the invisible work of preventing attacks. The pressure is mounting due to AI-enabled threats like Anthropic's Mythos model, which can autonomously discover vulnerabilities, increasing the workload and stress on security teams. However, the article also notes that 48% of cyber professionals are not threatened by AI replacing their jobs, as they see AI as a tool that increases the need for human oversight and strategic direction. The key takeaway for professionals is to upskill in AI, strategy, and communication to articulate security risks in business terms, thereby increasing their value and job satisfaction.

entities.name: Harvey Nash Global Tech Talent & Salary Report
entities.role: Survey provider that produced the data cited in the article

entities.name: Ankur Anand
entities.role: Group CIO at Nash Squared, providing commentary on the survey findings

entities.name: Anthropic's Mythos
entities.role: AI model that can autonomously discover vulnerabilities, cited as an emerging threat

key_data_points: 49% of cybersecurity professionals want to move jobs in the next 12 months, compared to 39% average across all tech roles (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: indicates high turnover in the field, which could affect team stability and knowledge retention; you may need to consider this when building or joining a red team).

Only 29% of cyber professionals received a pay increase in the past year, versus 56% in DevOps, 51% in product management, and 50% in business analysis (🟢 relevant to your career planning: highlights that cybersecurity may be undervalued financially, so negotiating skills and demonstrating business impact are crucial).

19% of organizations reported a major security attack in the past two years, yet boards often remain complacent because no breach occurred (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: this complacency can be exploited in social engineering or penetration testing to demonstrate real risk).

48% of cybersecurity professionals are not threatened by AI taking their jobs, as they see AI increasing the need for human oversight (🟢 relevant to your programming and Red Teaming: AI tools like Mythos can be used offensively to find vulnerabilities, but also defensively; you should learn to leverage AI in your own attacks and defenses).

The article emphasizes that the strongest cyber professionals combine technical depth with business context and communication skills (🟢 relevant to your ambition: developing these soft skills will make you more effective in red team engagements and career advancement).
conclusions: The cybersecurity field faces a retention crisis driven by inadequate compensation and recognition, despite increasing threats from AI. Professionals who adapt by acquiring AI, strategy, and communication skills will be better positioned to thrive. For red teamers, this means that while technical skills are essential, the ability to articulate findings in business terms and understand organizational dynamics is equally important for career growth and impact.

AI, particularly models like Anthropic's Mythos, introduces new vulnerabilities and attack vectors that red teamers can exploit. However, it also offers opportunities to automate reconnaissance and vulnerability discovery. Staying ahead requires continuous learning and integration of AI into your toolkit.

Organizations that fail to address security team burnout risk losing their best talent, creating openings for skilled professionals who can demonstrate value. As a student and aspiring red teamer, focusing on both technical excellence and strategic communication will set you apart in a competitive job market.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your cybersecurity and career interests. It highlights the current dissatisfaction in the field, which presents both a challenge and an opportunity: as a red teamer, you can differentiate yourself by not only mastering offensive techniques but also by learning to communicate risks in business language. The data on pay disparities and turnover underscores the importance of negotiating your worth and continuously upskilling, especially in AI. The article's emphasis on AI threats like Mythos directly ties to your red teaming needs—you should explore how such models can be used to automate vulnerability discovery. Additionally, the advice to develop strategic thinking aligns with your ambition to take initiative and advance quickly.
extra_advice: Consider building a personal project that integrates an AI model (like a local LLM or a vulnerability scanner) to automate the discovery of common vulnerabilities in a homelab environment. This will give you hands-on experience with AI-driven red teaming, a skill that is increasingly in demand. Also, practice presenting your findings to non-technical audiences (e.g., through blog posts or mock reports) to hone your communication skills—this will make you invaluable to future employers.
